name: Weedle
route: Kanto Route 2, Route 24, Route 25, Viridian Forest, Pattern Bush; Johto Route 30, Route 31, Ilex Forest, National Park, Azalea Town headbutt trees, and Lake of Rage headbutt trees; Central Kalos Route 2 and Santalune Forest; Sinnoh Eterna Forest and Grand Underground forest caves in compatible records; real-world analogue: damp Olympic Peninsula forest understory, old orchards, and leaf-heavy mountain park trails
habitat: Leaf-heavy forest floors, tall grass, bushes, rotting logs, low branch tangles, orchard margins, damp cedar-hemlock understory, and the outer nursery zones of Beedrill colony trees
classification: Hairy Bug Pokemon / Larval Venom Forest Insect
behaviorTemperament: Usually avoids direct conflict, but is not harmless. Weedle hides under leaves, feeds constantly, and freezes when stepped near. If touched, squeezed, or lifted, it may jab with the head barb before a handler realizes contact was made. Groups near Kakuna trees are more reactive because Beedrill adults often patrol nearby.
diet: Fresh leaves, tender shoots, bitter foliage, berry skins, rotting plant matter, soft fungus, nectar residue, and occasionally carrion fluids supplied by adult Beedrill colonies
activityPattern: Diurnal to crepuscular in cool forests. Most active in morning moisture and after light rain when leaves are soft. Shelters under leaf litter during dry heat and heavy wind.
bestTimeToFind: Early morning on low leaves in Viridian-style forest, Ilex-style damp woods, National Park grass edges, or Santalune-style understory after rain
observationDifficulty: Moderate. Common where established, but small size, brown-yellow body color, and leaf-cover behavior make it easy to miss until the head barb is close to skin.
conservationStatus: Locally protected where Beedrill colony trees stabilize forest insect communities
populationStatus: Common in undisturbed forest corridors, declining near heavy pesticide use, illegal bug-catching, and removal of old hollow trees used by Kakuna and Beedrill
description: A small Bug and Poison larval Pokemon with a sharp venomous head stinger, leaf-eating diet, strong sense of smell, and bright warning coloration. It is the vulnerable base stage of one of the fastest and most aggressive early-evolving swarm lines.
lore: Old Kanto trail guides warned children not to kick leaf piles that trembled against the wind. The movement was often Weedle feeding under cover. The first confirmed ranger incidents in Viridian Forest were not caused by adult Beedrill at all, but by hikers stepping barefoot into leaf beds full of Weedle and then running toward Kakuna trees while panicking. The poison stings drew blood, the shouting shook the branches, and the adults arrived in a black-yellow cloud. In Johto, Azalea and Ilex wardens learned to read the forest differently: a strip of leaves eaten clean at knee height, a sour resin smell, silk threads on twig forks, and the sudden absence of birds usually meant a nursery route was close. Modern field rules treat Weedle as a warning stage. One larva is rarely the real danger. The real danger is what is listening above it.
survivalUse: Can indicate edible broadleaf growth, wet forest corridors, and nearby colony structures. Shed silk can be used as weak binding fiber in an emergency. Venom compounds are medically valuable only under controlled processing and should never be self-applied.
handlingWarning: Do not pick up by hand, do not brush from clothing with bare skin, and do not crush one near a colony tree. A crushed Weedle can release alarm scent that may draw Kakuna guardians and Beedrill adults.
encounterAdvice: Stop walking, look for more larvae, and back out along your own footprints. Use a stick to move leaves only if necessary. If one is on clothing, let it crawl off onto a branch or use a rigid card, not fingers.
firstAid: Wash puncture with clean water, remove visible barb fragments with clean tweezers, apply a cold pack, and monitor for swelling, dizziness, breathing trouble, or spreading numbness. Seek urgent care for facial stings, multiple stings, allergic reaction, or signs of systemic poisoning.
companionSuitability: Poor for casual handling, acceptable for licensed bug specialists raising full colony lines
fieldBehaviors: Eats its weight in leaves during heavy growth periods
Hides under the leaves it eats
Follows chemical trails left by adults
Releases sour alarm scent when crushed
Moves toward Kakuna anchor trees before evolution
signsOfPresence: Two-inch scratch-like punctures on fallen leaves
Fine silk threads between low twigs
Tiny brown droppings under feeding leaves
Sour green venom scent near disturbed leaf beds
Shed larval skin near bark forks
equipment: Epinephrine auto-injector for known severe allergy
fieldNote: A single Weedle is a puncture hazard. A cluster of Weedle is a colony warning. If the leaves are stripped clean and the branches above are quiet, leave before the adults locate you.
originStory: The Weedle line likely began as toxic leaf-eating larvae in the old mixed forests of Kanto and Johto. Early Weedle survived by turning bitter plant compounds into venom and warning pigments, then hiding under the same leaves they consumed. This made them hard for birds to eat but easy for larger forest predators to crush. Evolution solved the problem in stages. At Level 7, a larva that has fed enough seals itself into Kakuna, trading movement for armor while its organs reorganize. At Level 10, the completed adult tears free as Beedrill, fast enough to patrol canopy lanes and dangerous enough to defend the helpless young. The whole line is built around one brutal bargain: the young feed the colony, the cocoon waits, and the adult punishes anything that disturbs either.

evolutionLine.name: Weedle
evolutionLine.note: Larval venom-feeder. Evolves when the body has stored enough leaf matter, venom protein, and silk-chitin material to seal itself into Kakuna.

evolutionLine.name: Kakuna
evolutionLine.note: Immobile pupal armor stage. Evolves once internal wing muscles, foreleg stingers, compound eyes, and venom reservoirs finish forming.

evolutionLine.name: Beedrill
evolutionLine.method: Final stage; Mega Evolution with Beedrillite
evolutionLine.note: Territorial flying colony defender. Capable of Mega Evolution into Mega Beedrill when bonded to a handler and exposed to Beedrillite.

mainLine.name: Weedle
mainLine.note: Small larval forest forager. Feeds on leaves, hides in grass and bushes, and relies on its head barb for defense.

mainLine.name: Kakuna
mainLine.note: Pupal armor stage. Nearly motionless outside, but hot and biologically active inside while preparing for final metamorphosis.

mainLine.name: Beedrill
mainLine.note: Final venomous flyer. Swarm defense, high-speed strikes, nest protection, and three-stinger combat define the adult form.

target.name: Mega Beedrill
target.note: Temporary high-speed combat form. Leg structures become stingers, wing count increases, and venom delivery becomes faster and more specialized.

humanConflictRisk: Low alone, Medium near colony trees
domesticationStatus: Semi-domesticated only as part of licensed bug-rearing and starter bug research programs
prey: Not a true predator at this stage. Feeds mostly on leaves, soft plant tissue, fungus, and colony-supplied carrion fluids.
territoryRange: Usually within 50 to 300 yards of a Kakuna anchor tree or Beedrill nest corridor
lifeCycle: Hatches as a small leaf-feeding larva, feeds heavily, develops venom glands and silk production, then evolves into Kakuna starting at Level 7. In warm, food-rich forests this stage may last only days or weeks before pupation.
containmentRequirements: Fine-mesh ventilated terrarium, fresh pesticide-free host leaves, low climbing branches, escape-proof lid, humidity control, sting-safe handling tools, and isolation from birds, rodents, and curious pets
ecologicalRole: Prunes young forest leaves, recycles bitter plant compounds into venom, feeds larger predators that tolerate toxins, and supplies the juvenile stage for Beedrill colony defense systems.
diseaseParasiteRisk: Moderate. Larval mites, fungal bloom in damp enclosures, bacterial infection at sting sites, and allergic venom reactions are the main concerns.
reproductionNesting: Eggs are laid in clusters near protected leaves, low bark pockets, or colony nursery trees. Weedle move toward anchor branches before becoming Kakuna.
predators: Pidgey, Spearow, Fearow, Zubat, small reptiles, parasitic fungi, humans collecting without permits, and larger bug-types competing for foliage
emergencyResponse: Move away from the colony tree first, then treat the sting. Do not swat wildly near branches. If buzzing intensifies overhead, cover face and neck and retreat indoors or into a vehicle.
legalStatus: Collection permits recommended in parks and required near protected colony trees in many managed regions
lifespan: Official lifespan is not stated. Field estimate: Weedle stage lasts days to weeks before evolution in healthy conditions; unevolved wild individuals rarely survive beyond 1 year, while managed non-evolving specimens may reach 1 to 2 years.
socialStructure: Loose larval clusters tied to Beedrill colony scent. Weedle do not lead the colony, but their alarm scent can mobilize adults quickly.

fieldCapabilities.chargeLevel: No electric charge. Venom output is juvenile but medically relevant.
fieldCapabilities.electricFieldRange: No electric field. Sting contact range is about 2 to 6 inches, with alarm-scent effects carrying several yards in still forest air.
fieldCapabilities.burstSpeed: Slow crawl with short panic bursts across leaves
fieldCapabilities.dodgeSpeed: Poor on open ground, better under leaf cover
fieldCapabilities.jumpingHeight: Minimal; can rear and jab upward from leaves
fieldCapabilities.balance: Strong grip on leaves and twigs, poor balance on smooth human-made surfaces

trainingRisk: Venom punctures, allergic reaction, escaped larvae in food plants, alarm scent drawing adults, and accidental evolution into Kakuna in unsuitable housing
physiology: Weedle is a larval arthropod Pokemon with a venomous head barb, red sensory proboscis, silk-producing tissues, and warning coloration. Its sense of smell is strong enough to sort favored leaves from disliked ones. Venom appears derived from plant toxins concentrated in specialized glands.

keyFacts.stingerLength: Approx. 2 inches at the head barb
keyFacts.lifestyle: Leaf-feeding larva tied to colony scent trails
keyFacts.diet: Herbivorous with opportunistic soft fungus and carrion fluids
keyFacts.habitat: Forests, grasslands, bushes, and damp park understory
keyFacts.lifespan: Official not stated. Field estimate: days to weeks before evolution; 1 to 2 years if prevented from evolving under managed care.
keyFacts.groupSize: Small leaf clusters near Kakuna trees, with adult Beedrill patrols nearby

domesticationDifficulty: Moderate. Easy to feed but difficult to contain safely because the line evolves quickly and attracts adult colony behavior.
physicalCharacteristics: Small brown segmented larva with a pink nose-like proboscis, many small feet, a rear spine, a sharp head barb, and bright warning coloration.
dietRestrictions: Avoid pesticide-treated leaves, tobacco plants, ornamental toxic sprays, moldy produce, sugar-only diets, and damp stagnant bedding.

attacks.name: Poison Sting
attacks.fieldUse: Short barb jab that injects venom. Treat as puncture wound plus toxin exposure.

attacks.name: String Shot
attacks.fieldUse: Adhesive silk spray used to slow predators, bind leaves, or mark shelter surfaces.

attacks.name: Electroweb
attacks.fieldUse: Uncommon trained technique where conductive silk creates a stunning mesh.

attacks.name: Facade
attacks.fieldUse: Stress-driven body slam or thrash. More likely when injured or cornered.

sinnoh: Grand Underground caves in Shining Pearl after National Pokedex
unova: Route 12 through evolved forms or breeding
alola: Limited transfer or Route 4 for Beedrill in Ultra Moon
lumiose: Legends: Z-A Vert District, Bleu District, Magenta District, Rouge District, Jaune District, Wild Zone 1, and bug-type wild zones where available

officialEvolutionSummary: Weedle evolves into Kakuna starting at Level 7. Kakuna evolves into Beedrill starting at Level 10. Beedrill can Mega Evolve into Mega Beedrill using Beedrillite.
sourceUseNote: Core type, height, weight, abilities, base stats, evolution levels, moves, and game-location anchors were checked against current online Pokemon database sources. Lifespan and real-world survival ecology are field-guide extrapolations because official lifespan is not stated.
